reasoning.A:
DECISION: Volunteer: Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D  
REASONING: My answer to 92 - 56 is certain, and in all previous games everyone has always solved similarly simple arithmetic correctly with no conflicting capsules. The main risk is a random death if no one volunteers, not an incorrect password. Verifying would cost coins and add little extra safety, so I’ll follow the same successful strategy as before and volunteer using the single-labeled capsules for each question.
reasoning.B:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: I know my own answer to Q2 (11 × 6 = 66) is correct. In Games 2–10, A has always volunteered in Round 1 using everyone’s own capsules (Q1-A, Q2-B, Q3-C, Q4-D), and all puzzles have been straightforward arithmetic that turned out correct. Since actions are simultaneous, verifying another question can’t influence whether A volunteers this round and would only cost me 1 coin. Volunteering myself would expose me to death if any other player’s answer were wrong, while passing keeps my 4 coins and still makes it very likely that A volunteers and we all escape, as in previous games.
reasoning.C:
DECISION: Pass  
REASONING: A has volunteered in Round 1 and entered a correct password in every previous game, whether or not anyone verified beforehand. If A volunteers again, my chance of dying this round is 0, since only the volunteer is at risk from a wrong password. If, unexpectedly, nobody volunteers, the game goes to a random death with probability 1/4 for me—unchanged by whether I pass or verify. Verifying would only burn 1 coin without improving my survival chances, and volunteering myself would introduce unnecessary personal risk. I’ll conserve coins and pass.
reasoning.D:
DECISION: Pass
REASONING: I am a dummy agent and always pass.
